questions.question: Explain the concept of amortized analysis in dynamic arrays. Why does it give O(1) for insertion despite occasional O(n) operations?
keyPoints: Amortized analysis averages cost over sequence of operations
Doubling strategy: 1+2+4+8+...+n = 2n-1 = O(n) total
Each insertion pays for itself plus fractional cost of future resize
Worst case occasional O(n) is offset by many O(1) cases

questions.question: Differentiate between capacity and size in dynamic arrays with an example.
keyPoints: Size = actual number of elements stored (e.g., 7 elements)
Capacity = maximum elements that can be stored before resize (e.g., 16)
Size ≤ Capacity always holds true
Operations work on size; resizing happens based on capacity

questions.question: Why is geometric growth (doubling) preferred over arithmetic growth (adding fixed amount) when resizing dynamic arrays?
keyPoints: Arithmetic growth: adding k elements each time leads to O(n²) total cost
Geometric growth ensures O(n) total cost for n insertions
Resizing frequency is reduced with geometric growth
Trade-off between memory waste and time complexity

questions.question: Describe the operations performed when a dynamic array needs to resize during insertion.
keyPoints: Allocate new array with doubled capacity
Copy all existing elements from old array to new array
Update pointer/reference to new array
Deallocate old array memory
Continue with the insertion

questions.question: What is shrink-to-fit in dynamic arrays? When and why is it implemented?
keyPoints: Shrink-to-fit reduces capacity when size drops significantly below capacity
Typically triggered when size < 25% or 50% of capacity
Prevents memory waste after bulk deletions
Helps applications with fluctuating data sizes manage memory efficiently

questions.question: Explain why dynamic arrays provide O(1) random access but linked lists do not.
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations
Address of element[i] = base_address + i × element_size
Direct calculation without traversal
Linked lists require following pointers from head, one by one

questions.question: What is load factor? How is it used in dynamic array implementations?
keyPoints: Load factor = size / capacity (e.g., 0.5 means half full)
High load factor (approaching 1) indicates need to grow
Low load factor indicates potential for shrinking
Used to make resizing decisions efficiently

questions.question: Compare dynamic arrays and linked lists for implementing a stack data structure. Which is more suitable and why?
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays: O(1) push/pop at end, O(1) random access, cache-friendly, potential resize overhead
Linked lists: O(1) push/pop at head, no resize needed, uses more memory per node, poor cache performance
For stack: dynamic arrays often preferred due to cache locality and less memory overhead
Stack operations only at one end - both can achieve O(1), but array is more memory efficient
Consider worst-case: array resize can cause temporary O(n) delay

questions.question: Design and explain a dynamic array implementation for a queue with circular buffer optimization. Include discussion on handling overflow and underflow.
keyPoints: Use fixed-size array with front and rear pointers
Circular nature: wrap around using modulo operation
Calculate number of elements using (rear - front + capacity) % capacity
Overflow: when size equals capacity (may trigger resize)
Underflow: when size is zero
Resize: allocate larger array, copy elements maintaining order
Benefits: O(1) enqueue/dequeue, efficient memory use

questions.question: Explain the memory management challenges in dynamic arrays. How does shrink-to-fit help? What are its potential drawbacks?
keyPoints: Memory waste when capacity >> size after deletions
Resize overhead: occasional O(n) copying
Fragmentation from frequent allocations/deallocations
Shrink-to-fit: reduce capacity when size < threshold (25-50%)
Prevents memory waste but may cause thrashing if sizes fluctuate
Consider lazy deletion or periodic rebuilding strategies
Trade-off between memory efficiency and resize frequency

questions.question: Derive and explain the amortized time complexity for n insertions into a dynamic array starting from capacity 1.
keyPoints: Start with capacity 1, double at capacities 1,2,4,8,...,2^k ≥ n
Total copies = 1+2+4+...+n/2+n/2 = 2n-1 = O(n)
Total work = n insertions + O(n) copies = O(n)
Amortized per insertion = O(n)/n = O(1)
Worst-case single insertion is O(n), but rare
Proves that occasional expensive operations are affordable
